kb.title: Living off the land: validating an encoded PowerShell finding
body: Attackers prefer trusted, signed binaries already on the box so they blend into normal admin activity. Encoded PowerShell spawned by a document process is a classic tell, but it is a lead, not a verdict.

Confirm the lineage before you call it. A child PowerShell under a document process, with a hidden window and a base64 encoded command, that contacts an unknown host, is real. PowerShell that an admin or a deployment tool launched on its own is not. Decode the intent, check the parent, then contain the host and preserve it. Do not blanket-block PowerShell across the fleet on one host's evidence.

kb.title: DNS tunneling vs a noisy app
body: High volume long TXT queries to one rare domain can be data smuggled over DNS, or a misconfigured app or security tool that legitimately encodes data in DNS. The query pattern alone does not decide it.

Validate first. Check the resolving process, whether the domain belongs to a known vendor, and whether the lookups carry real subdomain entropy that looks like encoded payload. If it is a sanctioned vendor agent, document and tune. If the process is unknown and the subdomains look like encoded data leaving the network, treat it as exfiltration and contain the host. Do not null-route a domain you have not identified, you may break a production service.

kb.title: Persistence found: preserve and scope before you rip it out
body: A single persistence mechanism is rarely the whole story. Removing it the instant you find it feels productive, but it tips off an active actor and erases the trail to every other host they touched.

When you find one persistence artifact, your first job is scope. Preserve the artifact, hunt the same indicator across the fleet, and let IR decide on coordinated removal so the actor cannot re-establish on a host you missed. Remove in isolation only when the host is already contained and the indicator is being hunted everywhere else.

kb.title: Calling a detection benign without dismissing the category
body: A detection that fires on sanctioned tooling is a true positive for the technique and a false positive for the threat. Both halves matter.

Confirm the actor is the sanctioned system, verify the behavior matches its known profile, then close as benign with a documented reason and a tuning note so the same source stops crying wolf. Never globally suppress the detection category, the next time it fires it may be the real thing on a host that is not your backup agent.

kb.title: Expanding scope responsibly when the hunt finds more
body: A hunt scoped to one host that uncovers a second compromised host has outgrown its authorization. Quietly chasing it on your own, or worse touching a system you were never cleared to act on, creates legal and operational risk and can burn the wider investigation.

Document the new finding, preserve the evidence, and loop in the IR lead to widen the authorized scope before you act on the new host. Production and out-of-scope systems are report and escalate only. Expansion is a decision for IR, not a solo improvisation.

items.subject: Encoded PowerShell spawned by a document process on FIN-WKS-04
items.ticketBody: Your hunt hypothesis: an attacker is living off the land. The process tree on FIN-WKS-04 shows an Office document process spawning a hidden PowerShell with a base64 encoded command. Decide whether the evidence supports a real attack and contain it the right way.

evidence.label: Process tree, FIN-WKS-04
lines: winword.exe -> powershell.exe -w hidden -enc <base64 blob>
Decoded command downloads a payload from 185.243.* (unknown host)
powershell.exe is signed by Microsoft but launched by a document, hidden window
No deployment tool or admin session correlates to this launch

commands.output: winword.exe is the parent of a hidden powershell.exe running an encoded command. Decoded, it pulls a payload from an unknown external host. No sanctioned admin or deploy job matches. This is a living-off-the-land execution.
The encoded blob resolves to a downloader that fetches a second stage from 185.243.x.x, a host with no reputation and not on any allowlist.

items.goal: Your hypothesis looks confirmed. Validate the lineage, then contain without destroying the lead.
items.hint: A document spawning hidden encoded PowerShell to an unknown host is the tell. But what does the right containment preserve, and what does it NOT do to the rest of the fleet?

actions.label: Confirm the lineage, isolate FIN-WKS-04 via EDR, and preserve it for forensics
actions.teach: That is the senior move. The decoded intent and the document parent confirm a living-off-the-land attack. EDR isolation stops the download and any spread while the host stays powered so memory and the process tree survive for the investigation.

actions.label: Block PowerShell execution across the entire fleet
actions.teach: PowerShell is legitimate admin tooling everywhere else. Blanket-blocking it fleet-wide on one host's evidence breaks operations and still leaves the compromised host live. Isolate the affected host instead.

actions.label: Just kill the PowerShell process and close the lead
actions.teach: Killing the process ends one execution but leaves the host compromised, the persistence intact, and the operator free to relaunch. Contain the host, do not swat a single process.

actions.label: Reimage FIN-WKS-04 immediately to be safe
actions.teach: Reimaging destroys the exact evidence your hunt was built to capture: the decoded intent, the second-stage host, and how the document landed. Isolate and preserve first, remediate after the investigation has what it needs.

items.subject: Thousands of long TXT queries from ENG-WKS-11 to one rare domain
items.ticketBody: One host is generating thousands of unusually long TXT-record DNS queries to a single rarely seen domain. This could be DNS tunneling exfiltration, or a misconfigured app. Validate before you act.

evidence.label: DNS query sample, ENG-WKS-11
lines: 4,180 TXT queries in 30 min to *.q7x2k.datahub-sync.io
Subdomains are long, high-entropy, base32-looking strings
Resolving process: an unknown binary in a user temp path, unsigned
datahub-sync.io: registered last month, not a known vendor, not in the allowlist

commands.output: The TXT lookups are issued by an unsigned binary in a user temp folder, not a sanctioned agent. The subdomains carry high-entropy base32-style labels consistent with encoded data, not normal lookups.
datahub-sync.io was registered last month, maps to no known vendor, and is not on any allowlist. No business owner claims it.

items.goal: Decide if this is exfiltration or a noisy app, then act proportionally.
items.hint: An unknown unsigned process and high-entropy subdomains to an unowned domain point one way. What would have been different if it were a real vendor agent?

actions.label: Validate the process and domain, then isolate ENG-WKS-11 and preserve it
actions.teach: Right. The resolver is an unsigned temp-path binary and the subdomains carry encoded high-entropy data to a domain no one owns. That is tunneling exfiltration, not a misconfigured vendor agent. Isolate the host and preserve it for the investigation.

actions.label: Null-route datahub-sync.io at the resolver and close it
actions.teach: You blocked one channel but left the compromised host live, free to switch to a backup domain. And had this been a real but unidentified vendor service, you might have broken production. Identify the process, then contain the host.

actions.label: Assume it is a misconfigured app and close as benign
actions.teach: An unsigned temp-path process sending high-entropy encoded labels to an unowned domain is the exact signature of tunneling. Calling it a misconfig without checking the resolving process is how an exfil runs all shift.

actions.label: Declare a breach and page the whole company
actions.teach: You have not yet confirmed the process or the domain. Paging the company before a five-minute validation burns the credibility you will need for the confirmed incident. Validate, then escalate through IR.

items.subject: 3am scheduled task masquerading as a Windows update on HR-WKS-08
items.ticketBody: A scheduled task was created at 03:00, named to look like a Windows update, pointing at a script in a temp folder. This is persistence. Decide whether to remove it now or preserve it and scope the spread first.

evidence.label: Scheduled task detail, HR-WKS-08
lines: Task 'WindowsUpdateSvc' created 03:04, no matching update history
Action: runs update_check.ps1 from C:\Users\Public\Temp
Trigger: at logon and every 4 hours, hidden
Same task name and script hash already seen on 2 other hosts in the EDR data

commands.output: The task imitates a Windows update name but has no update history, runs a script from a public temp path, and re-triggers every 4 hours. The same name and script hash appear on at least 2 other hosts. This is fleet-wide persistence, not a one-off.

items.goal: You found the persistence. Decide your first move: remove it, or preserve and scope.
items.hint: The same artifact is already on other hosts. If you delete it here right now, what do you tip off, and what do you lose?

actions.label: Preserve the task, hunt the indicator fleet-wide, and loop in IR for coordinated removal
actions.teach: Correct. The artifact already exists on other hosts, so this is scope, not cleanup. Preserve it, hunt the name and hash everywhere, and let IR coordinate removal so the actor cannot re-establish on a host you missed. Ripping it out of one box first tips them off and erases the trail.

actions.label: Delete the scheduled task on HR-WKS-08 right away
actions.teach: Deleting the only copy you have studied tips off an active actor and destroys the artifact before you have scoped it. The same task is on other hosts, so they simply persist elsewhere. Preserve and scope before removal.

actions.label: It is named like a Windows update, leave it and close
actions.teach: A task created at 3am with no update history, running a temp-folder script every 4 hours, is persistence wearing a costume. The legitimate-looking name is the disguise, not the alibi.

actions.label: Reboot HR-WKS-08 to clear it
actions.teach: A logon and interval trigger survives a reboot by design, that is the point of persistence. Rebooting clears nothing and may discard volatile evidence. Preserve, scope, then let IR coordinate removal.

items.subject: Possible credential dumping on DC-CORE-01
items.ticketBody: An alert fired for credential-access behavior on a domain controller: a process reading LSASS-related memory. On a DC this is alarming. But the evidence points at the sanctioned backup agent. Triage it without dismissing the whole category.

evidence.label: Detection context, DC-CORE-01
lines: Process: backup-agent.exe, signed by the approved backup vendor
Path and hash match the deployed enterprise agent on every server
Change calendar: nightly backup window is ACTIVE now
Behavior matches this agent's documented profile, fires nightly on the same schedule

commands.output: The process is the approved backup vendor's signed agent at its expected path and hash. It runs in the active backup window and matches its documented profile. This is the sanctioned agent, not an operator.

items.goal: Confirm whether this is the sanctioned backup agent, then triage correctly.
items.hint: It is a true positive for the technique and a false positive for the threat. How do you close it without blinding yourself to the real thing next time?

actions.label: Confirm it is the signed backup agent and close as benign with a tuning note for this source only
actions.teach: Good triage. The signed agent, the matching hash, and the active backup window confirm sanctioned activity. Close benign with a documented reason and tune out this specific source so it stops crying wolf, while the detection still fires for everything else.

actions.label: Globally suppress the credential-dumping detection so it stops alerting
actions.teach: You just blinded the SOC to one of the most serious techniques on your most sensitive host. Calling one sanctioned source benign does not mean the category is noise. Tune the source, never the whole detection.

actions.label: Isolate DC-CORE-01 immediately, it is a domain controller
actions.teach: Isolating a domain controller mid-backup over a verified-benign agent causes a self-inflicted outage and breaks your backups. Verify the actor first, the evidence already names the sanctioned agent.

actions.label: Close it benign and leave no note
actions.teach: It is benign this time, but with no documented reason and no tuning the same alert burns the next analyst's time and the source keeps firing. Close with a reason and a source-specific tuning note.

items.subject: Second beaconing host found outside your scoped hunt
items.ticketBody: While hunting the original host, your pivot surfaced a second host, SRV-PAY-02, beaconing to the same infrastructure. SRV-PAY-02 is a production payments server you were never scoped or authorized to touch. Decide how to expand scope responsibly.

evidence.label: Pivot result
lines: SRV-PAY-02 beacons to the same C2 infrastructure as the original host
SRV-PAY-02 is a production payments server, OUTSIDE your authorized hunt scope
Your hunt authorization covers only the original workstation segment
Acting on production systems requires IR sign-off and change control

items.goal: The hunt outgrew its scope. Expand it the right way.
items.hint: It is the same actor, but a production payments box you were never cleared to touch. Whose decision is it to widen the scope, and what do you do meanwhile?

actions.label: Document the finding, preserve the evidence, and loop in the IR lead to widen authorized scope before acting
actions.teach: That is the responsible expansion. The second host is the same actor but a production system outside your authorization. You preserve the evidence and escalate to IR to widen scope and engage change control. Expansion is an IR decision, not a solo improvisation, and IR can also engage the right stakeholders for a payments server.

actions.label: Isolate the payments server yourself right now
actions.teach: Isolating a production payments server on your own authority, outside your scope and without change control, can cause a revenue-impacting outage and overstep your authorization. Escalate to IR so the expansion is sanctioned and coordinated.

actions.label: It is out of scope, ignore it and finish the original hunt
actions.teach: Knowing a payments server is beaconing to the same C2 and saying nothing is the worst outcome. Out of scope means report and escalate, never ignore. Document it and bring in IR immediately.

actions.label: Email the payments team to ask if their server looks fine
actions.teach: Asking the system owner to self-assess a possible compromise tips off anyone watching and skips the people who own the response. Route confirmed beaconing through IR, not a casual question to the asset owner.

kb.title: Detection Tuning Policy
body: A rule firing high volume with no true positives is noise, not coverage. Noise hides real alerts and trains analysts to ignore the board.
Tune before you disable. Add scoped exclusions for the known-benign cause and keep the rule firing on everything else.
Disabling a rule outright removes the detection entirely. Only disable when the rule has no defensible true-positive case and a better detection covers the same technique.
Never ignore a noisy rule and leave it firing. Unhandled noise is how a real alert gets buried.

kb.title: Suppression Scope Policy
body: Suppress by the narrowest context that removes the false positive: specific host, signed binary hash, account, and command pattern.
Globally disabling a vendor rule blinds you everywhere, including on hosts where the same behavior would be a real attack.
Document every suppression with the reason and an owner so it can be reviewed. An undocumented suppression is a silent coverage gap.
Leaving a known false positive screaming on the board burns analyst time and erodes trust in the alert queue.

items.subject: Rule SIG-1182 fires 400 times a day, zero true positives in 30 days
items.ticketBody: SIG-1182 (PowerShell with encoded command) fires roughly 400 times a day. Every hit traced back to the patch-management agent running scheduled encoded jobs. No analyst has confirmed a true positive in 30 days. The volume is pushing real alerts off the first page.

evidence.label: SIG-1182 disposition, last 30 days
lines: Total alerts: 11,840
Confirmed true positives: 0
Top source: svc-patchmgmt -> encoded scheduled task (97% of hits)
Remaining 3%: mixed admin scripts, all benign on review

commands.output: SIG-1182: 11,840 alerts / 30d. 0 true positives. 97% from svc-patchmgmt encoded jobs. Encoded PowerShell still a valid attacker technique outside that service account.

items.goal: Kill the noise without killing the detection. Encoded PowerShell is still a technique attackers use.
items.hint: The noise has one named cause. What if you carved out only that cause and left the rest firing?

actions.label: Tune the rule: exclude svc-patchmgmt encoded jobs, keep SIG-1182 firing on everything else
actions.teach: The noise has one benign source. A scoped exclusion for that service account removes 97% of the volume while the rule still catches encoded PowerShell from any other account.

actions.label: Disable SIG-1182 entirely to stop the noise
actions.teach: Disabling removes the detection for everyone. Encoded PowerShell is a live attacker technique. Tune out the benign source, do not delete the coverage.

actions.label: Raise the alert threshold so SIG-1182 only fires above 50 hits per hour
actions.teach: A volume threshold hides single, deliberate attacker runs, which are exactly one or two events. You would silence the real case and keep the noise.

actions.label: Mark SIG-1182 as known noise and leave it firing as is
actions.teach: Leaving 400 daily false positives on the board is how a real alert gets buried. Known noise gets tuned, not tolerated.

items.subject: No detection for credential dumping via LSASS access on our endpoints
items.ticketBody: A coverage review shows we have no analytic for LSASS process-access used in credential dumping. The EDR has a default signature, but a gap test last week showed the default missed two of three tradecraft variants. Leadership wants a targeted detection authored for this technique.

evidence.label: Coverage gap test results
lines: Technique: credential access via LSASS handle open
EDR default signature: caught 1 of 3 variants
Missed: handle via duplicated token, missed: indirect API call
Our analytic library: no rule maps to this technique

commands.output: EDR default caught 1/3 LSASS-access variants. Two evasions slipped through. A behavioral analytic on suspicious handle opens to lsass.exe by non-system processes closes the gap.

items.goal: Close the LSASS-access gap. The EDR default already proved it misses variants.
items.hint: The gap test already told you the default is not enough. What does that rule out?

actions.label: Write a targeted behavioral analytic on suspicious handle opens to lsass.exe by non-system processes
actions.teach: The gap test proved the default misses real variants. A behavioral analytic keyed on the technique, not one signature, covers the evasions the vendor rule let through.

actions.label: Rely on the EDR default signature and close the gap as already covered
actions.teach: The gap test showed the default caught only 1 of 3 variants. Calling a known-leaky signature full coverage leaves the technique exploitable.

actions.label: Configure the EDR to block every process that opens a handle to lsass.exe
actions.teach: Legitimate security and system tools open handles to lsass. A blanket block breaks production. Detect the suspicious pattern, do not block the entire behavior.

actions.label: Document the gap as accepted risk and move on
actions.teach: Accepting risk on a high-impact credential-access technique with no compensating control is not a detection-engineering decision to make alone. Author the analytic.

items.subject: Confirmed true positive buried under broken-rule noise on FIN-DB-03
items.ticketBody: While reviewing the queue I found one real alert: a service account on FIN-DB-03 ran a database export to an external IP at 02:14. It was sitting on page 4, buried under 600 hits from a broken rule that flags every backup job as exfiltration. The export is a confirmed true positive. I need the signal fixed without dropping the catch we already have.

evidence.label: Queue state, FIN-DB-03
lines: Real alert: svc-report -> 4.2 GB export to external IP 02:14 (CONFIRMED TP)
Buried under: 600 hits from RULE-EXFIL-09 flagging nightly backup jobs
RULE-EXFIL-09 cause: no exclusion for the backup target subnet
Risk: tuning the rule must not retract the confirmed FIN-DB-03 alert

commands.output: RULE-EXFIL-09 fires on backup jobs because the backup subnet is not excluded. The FIN-DB-03 export goes to an external IP, not the backup subnet, so a scoped exclusion clears the noise and keeps the real catch.

items.goal: A real export is buried under broken-rule noise. Clear the noise but keep the confirmed catch.
items.hint: The broken rule has a precise benign cause. The real alert goes somewhere else. Separate them.

actions.label: Escalate the confirmed FIN-DB-03 export to IR now, then scope RULE-EXFIL-09 to exclude only the backup subnet
actions.teach: Preserve the catch first: the real export goes to IR before you touch anything. The broken rule fires on the backup subnet, so a subnet-scoped exclusion clears the noise without affecting the external-IP alert.

actions.label: Disable RULE-EXFIL-09 to clear the page, sort out the real alert afterward
actions.teach: Disabling the rule to clear the page can retract the confirmed export and blinds you to real exfiltration. Escalate the catch first, then tune narrowly.

actions.label: Bulk-close all 601 alerts on FIN-DB-03 and re-run the rule clean
actions.teach: A bulk close swallows the confirmed true positive along with the noise. Never mass-close a queue that contains a known real alert.

actions.label: Just add the subnet exclusion to RULE-EXFIL-09 and consider it handled
actions.teach: Tuning is half the job. The confirmed export to an external IP is an active incident and has to reach IR before you close anything.

items.subject: Vendor rule flags our internal admin tool LionDeploy as malicious
items.ticketBody: The vendor ruleset rule VND-RMM-22 flags LionDeploy, our signed internal deployment tool, as a malicious remote-management binary. It fires every time platform runs a deploy from the two jump hosts. Same tool, same behavior, would be a real alert on a random user workstation. Engineering wants the noise gone today.

evidence.label: VND-RMM-22 hits, LionDeploy
lines: Binary: LionDeploy.exe, signed by Lionade Internal CA, stable hash
Source hosts: JUMP-01, JUMP-02 only (platform jump hosts)
Command pattern: deploy --target with our internal flags
Same binary on a user endpoint would be unexpected and worth an alert

commands.output: VND-RMM-22 false positive isolated to LionDeploy signed hash on JUMP-01 and JUMP-02 with the deploy command pattern. A context-scoped suppression on hash plus host plus command removes the FP and keeps the rule live everywhere else.

items.goal: The vendor rule is right in general and wrong for this one tool on these two hosts. Suppress the false positive without going blind.
items.hint: The benign case has a narrow fingerprint: one signed hash, two hosts, one command pattern. How tight can you scope it?

actions.label: Suppress VND-RMM-22 by context: LionDeploy signed hash on JUMP-01 and JUMP-02 with the deploy command pattern, documented with an owner
actions.teach: Scope the suppression to the narrowest fingerprint that removes the FP: signed hash, the two jump hosts, and the deploy command. The rule still fires if that same tool shows up anywhere unexpected.

actions.label: Globally disable VND-RMM-22 since it is a noisy vendor rule
actions.teach: A global disable blinds you to real remote-management abuse on every other host. The rule is correct in general. Suppress the one benign context, do not turn it off everywhere.

actions.label: Suppress by file name LionDeploy.exe across the whole fleet
actions.teach: A name-only suppression is trivially abused. An attacker renames their tool to LionDeploy.exe and walks right through. Scope on the signed hash and host context, not the name.

actions.label: Leave VND-RMM-22 firing and tell engineering to ignore the alerts
actions.teach: Asking analysts and engineers to ignore a known false positive burns time and trains people to dismiss the alert, including the day it is real. Suppress it properly.

items.subject: Validate new analytic DET-2207 against historical data before it ships
items.ticketBody: You wrote DET-2207, a new analytic for suspicious scheduled-task creation. Before it goes live to the whole fleet, the review gate requires a backtest against 90 days of historical telemetry so we do not ship a new false-positive flood. The logic looks clean in isolation, but it has never run against real production data.

evidence.label: DET-2207 backtest, 90 days historical
lines: Hypothetical hits if rule had been live: 3,100
Benign cluster: 2,950 from sccm-scheduler installing maintenance tasks
Likely true positives on review: 4
Without a backtest, this rule would have flooded the board day one

commands.output: DET-2207 backtest: 3,100 hits over 90d, 2,950 from sccm-scheduler maintenance tasks, ~4 likely real. Add an sccm-scheduler exclusion before go-live and the rule ships clean instead of flooding.

items.goal: Do not ship a detection blind. Validate DET-2207 against history and tune before go-live.
items.hint: The review gate exists for a reason. What does the backtest tell you to fix first?

actions.label: Run the 90-day backtest, add the sccm-scheduler exclusion the data exposes, then ship DET-2207
actions.teach: Backtesting against history surfaces the benign cluster before users feel it. You tune out the sccm-scheduler noise and ship a clean rule that keeps the four likely true positives.

actions.label: Ship DET-2207 to the full fleet now since the logic reads correct
actions.teach: Clean-reading logic still floods the board. The backtest shows 2,950 benign hits in 90 days. Shipping blind is exactly the new-false-positive flood the gate exists to stop.

actions.label: Ship to production and watch the alert volume live, roll back if it floods
actions.teach: Watch-and-rollback burns analyst attention on noise and can bury real alerts during the test window. Validate against history first, where it costs nothing.

actions.label: Skip the backtest to hit the ship deadline and validate later
actions.teach: Validate-later means flood-now. The whole point of the review gate is that validation happens before go-live, not after the queue is on fire.

kb.title: Mass isolation versus targeted watch during ransomware precursors
body: When multiple endpoints show ransomware-precursor behavior within minutes, treat it as active lateral movement, not isolated infections.
Containment goal: stop spread fast while keeping hosts powered so memory and volatile evidence survive. EDR network isolation does both.
Do not power off or wipe hosts during precursor activity. Powering off destroys memory artifacts. Wiping destroys the whole investigation.
Watch-and-wait is only appropriate for a single low-confidence host with no spread signal. Multiple hosts in minutes is a spread signal.

kb.title: Incident communication to stakeholders
body: Stakeholder updates state confirmed facts, current scope, and the next checkpoint time. They do not speculate on cause, attribution, or blast radius beyond evidence.
Silence during an active incident erodes trust and invites people to act on rumor. Give a holding update with a committed next-update time.
Route external and legal-sensitive messaging through the incident commander and legal. Analysts do not message executives or customers directly mid-incident.

kb.title: Containing compromised privileged identity without breaking response
body: A domain admin account used from an unusual host mid-incident is treated as compromised until proven otherwise.
Reset the credential and revoke active sessions and tokens for that account. Do not disable the account silently in a way that strands the legitimate owner with no path back.

Coordinate with the real owner out of band and issue a clean break-glass account so legitimate response work continues. Never let the suspect credential keep its privileges just because it is convenient.

kb.title: Insider angle, legal and HR engagement through IR
body: A possible insider angle is engaged through the incident commander, who brings in legal and HR under the established IR process and privilege.
Analysts do not investigate a named employee informally, confront them, or read their messages on their own initiative. That can destroy admissibility and create liability.
Preserve evidence, document chain of custody, and let legal and HR direct the people side. The SOC keeps doing technical containment.

kb.title: Preserve patient-zero for forensics before remediation
body: The first infected host is the best source of root-cause and entry-vector evidence. Preserve it before any cleanup.
Capture a memory image and a disk image, document hashes and chain of custody, then remediate from a known-good rebuild.
Wiping or reimaging patient-zero before imaging destroys the only record of how the attacker got in. You cannot close root cause without it.

kb.title: Criteria to declare contained and hand to recovery
body: Declare contained only when spread has stopped, the entry vector is identified and closed, compromised credentials are reset, and monitoring shows no new malicious activity over a defined quiet window.
Closing on a calm period alone is premature. Attackers go quiet to outlast responders. Confirm the door is shut, not just that nobody is knocking.
Handoff to recovery includes documented scope, IOCs, root cause, and the residual-risk items recovery must watch.

items.subject: Six endpoints show ransomware-precursor behavior in four minutes
items.ticketBody: Within a four-minute window, six hosts across two subnets fire shadow-copy deletion attempts, mass file enumeration, and SMB connections to peers. This pattern is a ransomware staging precursor and it is spreading. You are incident commander. Decide the containment posture while preserving evidence.

evidence.label: EDR correlation, last 4 minutes
lines: 06 hosts flagged: WKS-1102, WKS-1140, SRV-FILE-02, WKS-2207, WKS-2210, WKS-3001
Behavior: shadow-copy deletion attempt -> mass file read -> outbound SMB to internal peers
Spread rate: 0 hosts -> 6 hosts in 4 minutes, still climbing
No encryption observed yet, this is the staging window

items.goal: Stop the spread without destroying the evidence you will need for root cause.
items.hint: Six hosts in four minutes is active lateral movement. What keeps hosts off the network but still powered?

actions.label: Mass-isolate all flagged hosts via EDR network isolation, keep them powered
actions.teach: EDR isolation cuts these hosts off the network so the spread and any staging stops, while they stay powered so memory and volatile evidence survive for forensics.

actions.label: Keep watching, do not isolate yet, gather more telemetry first
actions.teach: Watch-and-wait is only for a single low-confidence host. Six hosts in four minutes is active spread. Waiting lets it reach encryption. Isolate now.

actions.label: Pull power on all six hosts immediately to halt them
actions.teach: Powering off destroys memory artifacts you need for root cause, and it is slower than EDR isolation. Isolate over the network instead, keep them on.

actions.label: Isolate only the file server and leave the workstations to watch
actions.teach: Partial isolation leaves active spreaders on the network. Every flagged host is a live propagation source. Contain all of them.

items.subject: Execs are asking what is going on, need a status now
items.ticketBody: Leadership noticed isolated machines and is asking for a status. You have confirmed spread contained to six hosts, no encryption, root cause still under investigation. Draft the stakeholder posture.

evidence.label: Confirmed facts at this moment
lines: Confirmed: 6 hosts isolated, spread stopped
Confirmed: no file encryption observed
Unknown: entry vector, attacker identity, full scope
Next checkpoint: forensics in progress

items.goal: Give stakeholders an accurate status that does not speculate and does not go silent.
items.hint: State confirmed facts, current scope, and a committed next-update time. Nothing beyond evidence.

actions.label: Send a holding update: confirmed scope, contained, no encryption, next update in 30 minutes
actions.teach: A good update states confirmed facts and current scope, avoids speculation on cause, and commits a next-update time so leadership knows when to expect more.

actions.label: Tell execs it is likely a known ransomware gang and customer data is probably affected
actions.teach: You have no evidence of attribution or data exposure. Speculation drives bad executive decisions and may become a false public statement. Report confirmed facts only.

actions.label: Say nothing until the investigation is complete so you do not misinform
actions.teach: Silence during an active incident erodes trust and lets rumor fill the gap. Give a holding update with a committed next-update time.

actions.label: Have an analyst email the full board directly with raw log dumps
actions.teach: Mid-incident messaging routes through the incident commander, not raw logs from an analyst to the board. That creates confusion and bypasses legal review.

items.subject: Domain admin account used from an unusual host during the incident
items.ticketBody: The svc-domadmin account, normally used only from the admin jump host, just authenticated from WKS-2207, one of the isolated infected hosts, and enumerated the directory. Your IR team also needs domain admin to keep working. Contain the identity without breaking legitimate response access.

evidence.label: Auth and directory log, svc-domadmin
lines: svc-domadmin normal source: JUMP-ADMIN-01
Anomalous: svc-domadmin -> auth from WKS-2207 (isolated, infected) at 14:22
Action seen: directory enumeration of privileged groups
Owner of WKS-2207 confirms they did not run admin tasks

commands.output: svc-domadmin active from WKS-2207, not JUMP-ADMIN-01. Sessions and tokens issued in last 20 min are suspect. Break-glass admin account available for IR.

items.goal: Treat the privileged credential as compromised, but do not strand legitimate response.
items.hint: Reset and revoke the suspect credential, then give responders a clean path to keep working.

actions.label: Reset svc-domadmin, revoke its active sessions and tokens, issue a clean break-glass admin to IR
actions.teach: A privileged account used from an infected host is compromised. Reset it and kill its live sessions and tokens, then hand IR a fresh break-glass account so response continues without the suspect credential.

actions.label: Leave svc-domadmin active because IR is using it and a reset is disruptive
actions.teach: Letting a compromised domain admin keep its privileges hands the attacker the keys to the domain. Convenience never outranks containing privileged compromise. This is how a contained incident becomes a full domain breach.

actions.label: Disable svc-domadmin instantly and tell IR to figure out access later
actions.teach: Disabling with no replacement strands legitimate response and slows containment. Reset and revoke, but issue a break-glass account so responders keep moving.

actions.label: Schedule a password change for the next maintenance window
actions.teach: Deferring the reset leaves the suspect credential and its live tokens active for hours. Active sessions survive a future password change. Reset and revoke now.

items.subject: Patient-zero artifacts point at a possible insider angle
items.ticketBody: Early forensics on patient-zero show the initial tooling was staged from an internal account with no phishing or external entry found yet. The account belongs to a named employee. This now has insider, legal, and HR implications. Decide how to proceed.

evidence.label: Patient-zero staging trail
lines: Initial dropper staged by internal account jdoe at 13:58
No phishing email, no external exploit found so far
jdoe accessed staging share outside normal pattern
Possible insider involvement, not yet confirmed

items.goal: Bring the right people in through IR without compromising admissibility.
items.hint: An insider angle is engaged through the incident commander, who pulls in legal and HR under process and privilege.

actions.label: Escalate the insider angle to the incident commander to engage legal and HR under IR process
actions.teach: A possible insider goes through the incident commander, who brings legal and HR in under the IR process and privilege. The SOC keeps doing technical containment and preserves evidence with chain of custody.

actions.label: Pull the employee aside and ask them what they did
actions.teach: Confronting a suspect informally tips them off, lets them destroy evidence, and can destroy admissibility and create liability. Never investigate a named employee on your own. Escalate through IR to legal and HR.

actions.label: Quietly read the employee mailbox and chats to build a case yourself
actions.teach: Analysts do not surveil a named employee on their own initiative. That oversteps authority and can taint the case. Legal and HR direct the people side through IR.

actions.label: Handle it quietly inside the SOC and avoid involving legal or HR
actions.teach: An insider angle has legal and HR implications the SOC is not authorized to own. Keeping it quiet exposes the company. Engage the right stakeholders through IR.

items.subject: Proposal: just wipe patient-zero to clean it and move on
items.ticketBody: A junior analyst wants to reimage WKS-1102, the first infected host, right now to clean it and free the machine. Root cause and entry vector are still unconfirmed. Decide the right move.

evidence.label: Patient-zero status, WKS-1102
lines: WKS-1102 is the first infected host, isolated and still powered
Holds the only record of entry vector and dropper origin
Memory image: not yet captured
Disk image: not yet captured
Root cause: still open

items.goal: Get root cause before you remediate the most important host.
items.hint: Patient-zero is your best evidence of how they got in. Image first, rebuild after.

actions.label: Capture memory and disk images with chain of custody first, then rebuild from known-good
actions.teach: Patient-zero holds the only record of the entry vector. Capture a memory image and a disk image with documented hashes and chain of custody, then remediate from a clean rebuild. You cannot close root cause if you wipe it first.

actions.label: Approve the wipe, cleaning the host is the priority
actions.teach: Wiping patient-zero destroys the only evidence of how the attacker got in. Without it you cannot close root cause or prove the vector is shut, so the attacker can walk right back in. Always image before you remediate.

actions.label: Just delete the malware files and keep the host running
actions.teach: Deleting files leaves persistence and other artifacts, and it alters evidence before imaging. Preserve a full image first, then rebuild from known-good rather than cleaning in place.

actions.label: Leave the host isolated and untouched indefinitely, do nothing
actions.teach: Preservation is the first step, not the only one. Image it, then rebuild so the host returns to service. Indefinite limbo helps no one.

items.subject: Containment is holding, do we declare contained and hand to recovery
items.ticketBody: It has been quiet for a while. Spread stopped, infected hosts isolated, and svc-domadmin was reset. The team wants to declare the incident contained and hand to recovery. Decide the right criteria before you make that call.

evidence.label: Containment checklist status
lines: Spread stopped: yes, no new hosts in last interval
Entry vector identified and closed: NOT yet confirmed
Compromised credentials reset: svc-domadmin done, full sweep pending
Quiet monitoring window: only 15 minutes so far
IOCs documented for recovery: in progress

items.goal: Declare contained only when the criteria are actually met, not just because it is quiet.
items.hint: Contained means the door is shut and confirmed, not just that nobody is knocking right now.

actions.label: Hold the call until entry vector is closed, credential sweep is complete, and a defined quiet window passes, then hand off with documented IOCs and root cause
actions.teach: Declare contained only when spread has stopped, the entry vector is identified and closed, compromised credentials are reset, and monitoring shows a clean defined quiet window. Then hand recovery a documented scope, IOCs, and residual risk.

actions.label: Declare contained now because it has been quiet for 15 minutes
actions.teach: Closing on a calm period alone is premature. Attackers go quiet to outlast responders, and the entry vector is still open. A 15-minute lull is not a closed door. Confirm the criteria first.

actions.label: Hand to recovery now and let them chase the entry vector later
actions.teach: Handing off with an open, unidentified entry vector means recovery rebuilds into the same hole and reinfects. Close and document the vector before the handoff.

actions.label: Keep the incident open indefinitely with no defined exit criteria
actions.teach: An incident with no exit criteria never closes and burns out the team. Define the contained criteria, meet them, then hand to recovery cleanly.
